result:
The first thing I notice is the humming of the refrigerator, a sound so constant it’s become the silence I think in. It’s 3:07 a.m. The stove clock glows green like a single eye that refuses to blink. I am on the kitchen floor because the tiles are cool and because the ceiling looks farther away from here, as if the apartment has gained an extra story while I wasn’t measuring. Someone on another floor flushes a toilet; water rushes through the walls like a captive river that has forgotten the sea. I press my cheek to the grout and try to remember when I last felt indebted to my own pulse.  

There is a theory that every person owns one thousand words that will never be spoken or written, a secret personal lexicon burning holes in the pockets of the lungs. Tonight I am spending mine lavishly, whispering them to the chlorine-smelling darkness: apricity, the warmth of sun in winter; ellipsism, the sadness of never knowing how history will turn out; kummerspeck, the weight gained from sorrow. The words leave my mouth like small white moths and dissolve against the oven door. I picture them drifting up the vent, entering someone else’s sleep as the faint taste of iron or the sudden urge to call an estranged sister.  

I once loved a woman who collected burnt-out lightbulbs. She kept them in a shoebox under the bed like fragile urns, each filament a curled gray deity. When I asked why, she said, “Because they held the dark at bay and paid with their lives; the least we can do is bury them at sea.” We never made it to any coast; instead we buried the box in the community garden behind the library, between the compost bin and the rhubarb. A week later the city rototilled the plot. I imagine the tines catching on glass, the soft pop of vacuum giving way to soil, and somewhere underground a constellation of argon gas making a home for worms.  

My father believed that houses remember in their joints. He talked to the studs when he opened walls to run new wire, greeting them like old soldiers: “Still holding the line, I see.” After he died we sold the place to a couple who painted every room the color of wet sand. Six months later they invited us back for a barbecue. I stepped inside and felt the rooms shy, as if the walls had been embarrassed into forgetting. But when I rested my palm against the doorframe I could feel a faint electrical hum, the echo of my father’s voice saying Carry on, carry on, the way he used to when he screwed in a breaker and flipped it live.  

Memory is a photocopier that improves the contrast each time: the blacks blacker, the whites until they scald. I remember my mother’s tomato plants as tall as carnival giants; in the photograph they barely reach her waist. I remember the smell of the cafeteria in kindergarten—boiled hot dogs and fear—but when I visit the school now (converted into condos with Edison bulbs and faux-exposed brick) the hallway is vanilla and teriyaki from the fusion place next door. The mind keeps a private soundtrack, unreleased remixes with the bass boosted until the floor vibrates under the bed where you hid during thunderstorms.  

At 3:22 the streetlights outside click off as if someone pulled the cord on a cosmic vending machine. The sudden removal of orange makes the window a mirror, and I see myself sprawled like a starfish that lost interest in the tide. There is a notebook on the counter above me, closed, containing a single sentence I wrote yesterday: “The future is a library fine I keep renewing because I’m afraid to finish the book.” I think about opening the notebook again, adding: “But tonight I read with the flashlight of the body, batteries made of liver and regret.” Instead I leave it shut; some prophecies are better left half-eaten by their own envelope.  

I try to recall the taste of 1998. It is bubble-gum toothpaste, chlorine, and the metallic tang of a cassette whose ribbon has been rewound too many times. I can still perform the ritual: pencil through the spool, wind wind wind, the ribbon tightening like a secret wedding ring. Inside that plastic shell lived a song about love that was mathematically impossible—six verses, three choruses, no bridge—yet we slow-danced to it in somebody’s basement while their parents upstairs rehearsed the choreography of divorce. When the tape inevitably snarled, we pulled the crumpled brown ribbon across the room, a clumsy maypole, and laughed because we didn’t yet know that disasters could be private.  

Now disasters are public and high-definition, edited into vertical slices we scroll through while waiting for the kettle. I sometimes mute the audio and supply my own soundtrack: the hiss of the hospital vending machine that dropped two bags of pretzels the night my daughter learned to breathe without me. The crack of melting ice on the river the afternoon I decided to stay alive. The exact frequency at which a heart breaks—33 kilohertz, felt more than heard, like the dog whistle of childhood calling you home to a house that has since been sanded smooth of any evidence you ever lived there.  

I read somewhere that the universe is flat like a paper plate, and if you travel far enough you end up back at your own backyard but upside down. I picture another version of me walking across the kitchen ceiling, barefoot against the plaster, looking down/up at this me on the tiles. We wave. One of us is smiling; the other is weeping; the difference is only a matter of gravity. I want to ask ceiling-me whether the lightbulbs there never burn out, whether the tomatoes grow downward into clouds, whether our father’s voice still hums in copper bones of walls. But words cost coins I no longer carry, so we continue orbiting each other like moons of a planet neither of us has named yet.  

The refrigerator cycles off. In the hush I hear my pulse counting inventory: thirty-seven years, three months, two days, fourteen hours. Stock remaining: one heart, moderately dented. Pair of lungs, still under warranty. Dreams: discontinued, but some recalled models rumored on backorder. I think of the thousand words again and realize I’ve miscounted; I have one left. It rises now, a slow balloon against the ribs. I do not shape it with tongue or teeth; I let it hover, luminous, between the floor and the ceiling, between the two mes, between the moment and the minute that will soon arrive with its broom to sweep me into morning.  

The word is stay.  

It is not for the ceiling twin, nor for the ghosts of bulbs and tomatoes. It is for the tile under my shoulder blade cooling like a small flat moon. For the heartbeat that persists like a neighbor vacuuming at midnight—annoying, steadfast. For the notebook sentence that wants an ending. For the humming that will start again any second now.  

Stay, I breathe, and the refrigerator answers by clicking back on, a small applause. The green clock blinks 3:59, then, without drama, 4:00. Outside, somewhere, a trash truck begins its digestive song. The world exhales recyclables and grinds yesterday into tomorrow. I stand up, knees creaking like old chairs taking offense. I fill a glass of water and drink the future, lukewarm with a hint of pipe. I open the notebook. The sentence waits, patient as a dog who knows the walk is coming. I add three words: “But I will.” The page looks back, eyes wide, ready to be walked into daylight.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
